title: The part that becomes invisible to avoid being a target
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The child who learned to disappear 

 I remember exactly where I was sitting the first time I realized I had become invisible. It was a Tuesday afternoon....
htmlContent:
The child who learned to disappear

I remember exactly where I was sitting the first time I realized I had become invisible. It was a Tuesday afternoon. I was 14. My father was yelling again about something I couldn't even remember. And I was sitting on the floor of my bedroom, knees pulled to my chest, staring at the crack in the wall where the plaster had started to crumble. And I felt nothing. No fear. No anger. No sadness. Just this strange hollow stillness, like I had become part of the furniture.

Here's the thing. That feeling of disappearing wasn't a defect. It was a survival strategy. A brilliant one, actually. When you grow up in a home where being seen means being targeted, where having a need means being shamed, where expressing an emotion means being punished, you learn fast. The part of you that wants things, that feels things, that has opinions and preferences and desires, that part has to go underground. It has to become invisible. Because visibility equals danger.

I see this in so many of the people I work with. Clients who can tell you everything about everyone else in their life but have no idea what they themselves want for dinner. People who can for hours of other people's trauma but collapse when asked "How are you feeling right now?" Partners who have spent decades in relationships where their own needs were never spoken because they learned, somewhere deep in their bones, that having needs makes you a target.

This part of you, the one that learned to disappear, isn't a weakness. It's not a flaw. It's not something to be ashamed of. It's a protector. It kept you alive. It kept you safe. And it's still doing its job, even if the danger is long gone. Even if you're now in a safe relationship, living in a safe home, surrounded by safe people. That part doesn't know that. It's still scanning for threats. Still making sure you don't get too visible. Still whispering, "Stay small. Stay quiet. Stay safe."

Think about it. When was the last time you felt truly seen by someone and didn't immediately want to run and hide? When was the last time someone praised you and you didn't feel a wave of nausea or a desperate urge to deflect? When was the last time you asked for what you needed without apologizing, explaining, or minimizing?

If you're like most survivors I know, those moments are rare. And that's not your fault. That's the part. The part that became invisible to avoid being a target.

How the invisibility program installs itself

Peter Levine, the trauma researcher who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a memory in your head. It's a physical response pattern. A nervous system that has learned to brace for impact. A body that knows how to freeze, collapse, and disappear long before your conscious mind even registers the threat.

I remember doing a body scan in my therapist's office maybe six years ago. She asked me to notice where I felt sensation in my body. I said, "Nowhere." She asked me to try again, slower. I said, "I don't feel anything." She asked me to put my hand on my chest. I did. And I realized my chest was completely tight. Rock hard. Like there was a steel plate covering my heart. And I had been walking around like that for decades without even noticing.

That's how invisibility works. It's not just a psychological strategy. It's a full-body survival response. Your muscles learn to hold tension in specific patterns. Your breath becomes shallow. Your eyes learn to look down, to avoid eye contact, to scan for exits. Your voice gets quieter. Your opinions get smaller. Your needs disappear into a fog of "I don't care, whatever you want is fine."

Janina Fisher, who trained under Bessel van der Kolk and specializes in structural dissociation, describes how the traumatized self gets split into parts. There's the part that holds the pain, the part that fights, the part that flees, and the part that freezes. The invisible part is often a freeze response. It's the part that learned that if you don't move, don't speak, don't exist, the predator might not see you. It's the part that still believes, on a cellular level, that being seen could get you killed.

And here's the cruel irony. The same strategy that kept you safe as a child becomes the thing that keeps you trapped as an adult. The invisibility that protected you from abuse now prevents you from being loved. The disappearing act that saved you from punishment now starves you of connection. The silence that kept you out of trouble now makes it impossible for anyone to truly know you.

I think about this a lot. How the very thing that saved us is now what's breaking us. How the protector becomes the prison. How the part we needed so desperately to survive is the part we now need to heal.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying you should fire that part. I'm not saying you should force yourself to become visible overnight. That would be retraumatizing. That would be asking your nervous system to do something it knows is dangerous without giving it any reason to feel safe. What I'm saying is that you can start to notice this part. You can start to understand its logic. You can start to appreciate how hard it's been working, how many years it's spent keeping you safe, how much it's sacrificed so you could survive.

And then, maybe, you can start to show it that the danger is over. Slowly. Gently. One small moment of visibility at a time.

The cost of staying invisible

Let me be direct about something. The wellness industry loves to talk about "finding your voice" and "stepping into your power" and "owning your truth." And all of that sounds great. But if you have a part of you that learned to become invisible to survive, hearing those phrases might make you want to throw up. Because the message underneath is, "You're broken and you need to fix yourself." And that's not helpful. That's just more shame.

Here's what's actually true. Staying invisible costs you. It costs you relationships where you're truly known. It costs you work that reflects your actual talents and desires. It costs you the experience of being loved for who you really are, not for the small, quiet, agreeable version of yourself that you've been performing.

I spent years in relationships where I was the "easy" partner. The one who never complained. The one who always said yes. The one who adjusted to everyone else's needs. And I was so proud of that. I thought I was being a good person. I thought I was being loving. But I wasn't. I was being invisible. And the people who loved me couldn't actually love me because they didn't know who I was. They were loving the part. The mask. The performance.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman. She talks about how trauma survivors often lose the capacity to feel their own emotions and know their own needs. The invisibility part is a direct result of that. When your emotions were dangerous, you learned to shut them down. When your needs were punished, you learned to extinguish them. And now, as an adult, you might not even know what you feel or what you need. Because that part of you has been gone so long it feels like it never existed.

Wild, right? You can spend 30 years thinking you're fine, that you don't have any issues, that your childhood wasn't that bad, that you're just an easygoing person. And then one day you're sitting in a therapist's office and you realize you can't answer the question "What do you want?" because you genuinely don't know. There's nothing there. Just static. Just the echo of a child who learned that wanting things was dangerous.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson. She explains how children of emotionally immature parents learn to make themselves small to avoid becoming targets. They learn to read the room, to anticipate needs, to disappear into the background. And they carry that invisibility into every relationship they have for the rest of their lives.

The cost of staying invisible is that you never get to find out who you actually are. You never get to know what you actually want. You never get to experience what it feels like to be loved for your real self, because your real self has been in hiding for so long that even you don't know where it is.

Starting to be seen, one small moment at a time

I'm not going to tell you that healing the invisible part is easy. It's not. It's terrifying. Every time you show a piece of your real self to someone, your nervous system is going to scream at you. It's going to tell you that you're in danger. It's going to try to pull you back into hiding. That's normal. That's the part doing its job.

But here's what I've learned. The antidote to invisibility is not grand declarations or dramatic transformations. It's small, consistent acts of visibility. It's saying what you actually want for dinner when someone asks. It's telling your partner that you need some space instead of saying "I'm fine" when you're not. It's letting yourself cry in front of someone you trust. It's admitting that you're struggling instead of saying "I'm good" automatically.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab. She talks about how boundaries aren't about pushing people away. They're about letting people know who you actually are. And for the invisible part, boundaries feel terrifying because they require visibility. They require you to say, "This is what I need. This is what I won't accept. This is who I am."

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz. It gives you concrete exercises for reconnecting with your body and your emotions. For learning to tolerate the sensation of being seen. For slowly, carefully, bringing the invisible part back into the light.

I want to be honest with you about something. I still struggle with this. I still have days where I feel the old pull to disappear. Where I catch myself making myself small in a conversation. Where I realize I've been holding my breath for the last 10 minutes. Where I notice that I've been agreeing with someone just to avoid conflict, even though I disagree with every word they're saying.

But I also notice those moments now. And sometimes, not always, but sometimes, I choose differently. I take a breath. I let myself be seen. I say the thing I'm afraid to say. And my nervous system screams at me. And I survive. And the next time, it screams a little less. And I survive again. And slowly, over time, the invisible part starts to learn that being seen is not the same as being a target.

You don't have to do this alone. You don't have to force yourself. You don't have to be brave or strong or anything else. You just have to be willing to notice the part. To thank it for keeping you safe. And to show it, one small moment at a time, that the world has changed.

And maybe, eventually, the invisible part can learn to trust that it's okay to be seen.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab
  • Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson
